title: Cat Pink Frothy Nasal Discharge: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: If your cat has pink frothy nasal discharge, this is a veterinary emergency. Learn about pulmonary edema, urgency levels, and immediate next steps.
content_html:
What is Pink Frothy Nasal Discharge?

Pink, bubbly, or frothy discharge from a cat's nose is a serious clinical sign usually associated with fluid in the lungs, known as pulmonary edema. The pinkish tint indicates the presence of blood, while the frothy texture is created when air and fluid mix within the respiratory tract. This often occurs when the heart cannot pump efficiently or if there has been severe trauma or toxin exposure.

Is it an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening emergency. Pink frothy discharge suggests your cat is literally drowning from internal fluid buildup and is in severe respiratory distress. You should not wait for an appointment or observe the cat at home. Immediate oxygen therapy and veterinary intervention are required to save the cat's life.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you can safely do so without delaying your departure to the emergency clinic, take a quick photo of the discharge and your cat’s breathing posture. Showing this to the triage technician upon arrival allows them to immediately categorize the severity of the respiratory crisis. Seeing the exact color and bubble consistency helps the vet differentiate between upper respiratory infections and more critical pulmonary issues before diagnostic imaging even begins.

faqs.question: Can I give my cat any over-the-counter medicine for this?
faqs.answer: No. Never give a cat human medications or respiratory treatments. This condition requires professional oxygen therapy and diuretics that only a veterinarian can provide safely.

faqs.question: What are common causes of pink frothy fluid?
faqs.answer: The most common cause in cats is congestive heart failure. Other causes include electrocution (chewing on cords), severe pneumonia, or blunt force trauma to the chest.

faqs.question: What should I do on the way to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the cat in a carrier, minimize stress, and keep the car cool. Do not try to clean the nose if it causes the cat to struggle, as any extra stress increases their oxygen demand.
